groups.description: The rules of correct English — tenses, agreement, articles — and how to spot and fix mistakes.

topics.description: Parts of speech, tenses, articles, prepositions and subject-verb agreement.

videos.label: All 12 English verb tenses — complete lesson
videos.note: Clear structure + examples for every tense

learn.realLife: Grammar is the set of traffic rules for English: it decides which word goes where, which verb form matches the subject, and which little words (a, an, the, in, on, at) hold a sentence together. Exams don't ask you to define grammar — they ask you to USE it: pick the right verb, article or preposition so the sentence reads correctly. A handful of high-frequency rules cover the vast majority of questions.
learn.coreIdea: The core rules you need are: (1) Subject–verb agreement — a singular subject takes a singular verb ('he goes', not 'he go'); (2) Tenses — the verb form must match the time (past/present/future) and whether the action is ongoing or complete; (3) Articles — 'a' before a consonant SOUND, 'an' before a vowel SOUND, 'the' for something specific; (4) Prepositions — fixed pairings like 'good AT', 'married TO', 'depend ON'; (5) Pronouns — must agree with what they replace. Get these right and most sentence questions fall into place.
learn.howItWorks: Think of each rule as a check you run on the sentence. Agreement: match the number of the subject to the verb (ignore words between them — 'The box of apples IS heavy'). Tense: identify the time marker (yesterday → past, since/for → present perfect, tomorrow → future). Articles: judge by SOUND, not spelling ('an hour', 'a university'). Prepositions: memorise the common fixed combinations. Modifiers: keep them next to the word they describe. Parallelism: items in a list share the same form ('reading, writing and singing').

rules.name: Subject–verb agreement
rules.explanation: Ignore phrases between subject and verb.

rules.name: Articles by sound
rules.explanation: Judge by pronunciation, not the first letter.

rules.name: Present perfect
rules.explanation: Links past action to now.

rules.name: Fixed prepositions
rules.rule: good AT, married TO, depend ON, differ FROM
rules.explanation: These pairings are set by usage.

rules.name: Parallel structure
rules.explanation: All items match in form (all -ing, all nouns…).

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Subject–verb agreement
workedTypes.idea: Match number. What makes it different: distracting phrase between subject and verb.
workedTypes.problem: Choose the correct verb: 'The bunch of keys ___ on the table.' (is/are)

steps.why: The verb agrees with the head noun.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Correct tense
workedTypes.idea: Match the time marker. What makes it different: 'since/for' signals present perfect.
workedTypes.problem: Choose the correct form: 'They ___ in Delhi since 2015.' (live/have lived)

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Article choice
workedTypes.idea: Judge by sound. What makes it different: spelling can mislead.
workedTypes.problem: Choose the article: 'She is ___ honest person.' (a/an)

steps.action: 'honest' begins with a vowel SOUND (the 'h' is silent).

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Preposition
workedTypes.idea: Use the fixed pairing. What makes it different: memorised combination.
workedTypes.problem: Choose the preposition: 'He is married ___ a doctor.' (with/to)

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Parallel structure
workedTypes.idea: Match forms. What makes it different: list items must share form.
workedTypes.problem: Correct the sentence: 'She likes to swim, to run and dancing.'

steps.action: The list mixes 'to swim/to run' with 'dancing'.
Make it 'to swim, to run and to dance'.

workedTypes.answer: '...to swim, to run and to dance.'

traps: Matching the verb to the nearest noun instead of the real subject.
Choosing 'a/an' by first letter rather than by sound ('an hour', 'a university').
Using simple past with 'since/for' instead of the present perfect.
Guessing prepositions instead of learning the fixed pairings.
Breaking parallel structure in a list of items.
learn.memoryHook: Run five checks: Agreement (subject↔verb), Tense (time marker), Article (by sound), Preposition (fixed pair), Parallelism (same form). Most questions fail exactly one of these.

crack.oneLine: Most grammar questions test one of five things — subject–verb agreement, tense, articles (by sound), fixed prepositions, and parallel structure — so scan the sentence for which single rule is being broken.

strategies.name: Agreement check
strategies.method: Strip phrases between subject and verb, then match number.

strategies.name: Time-marker tense
strategies.method: Let since/for → present perfect, yesterday → past, tomorrow → future.

strategies.name: Sound-based article
strategies.method: 'an' before vowel SOUND, else 'a'.

strategies.name: Fixed pairs
strategies.method: Recall the set preposition.

signalGuide.signal: long subject phrase before the verb

crack.whyItWorks: Standard English fixes the relationship between a subject and its verb, between a time marker and a tense, and between certain words and their prepositions. Checking these fixed relationships turns a vague 'which sounds right' into a reliable rule test.
bestFor: Fill-in-the-blank grammar, verb/article/preposition choice.
Sentences built to test one clear rule.
notFor: Style or nuance questions with more than one acceptable answer.
Idiomatic usage that breaks the general rule.
crack.practicePattern: Every grammar question: identify the ONE rule in play (agreement, tense, article, preposition, parallelism) and apply it.

topics.description: Find the grammatical error in a given sentence.

videos.label: Spot the Error — Prepositions (CareerRide)
videos.note: Omission/misuse of prepositions with practice sentences

learn.realLife: Error spotting is like proofreading a friend's message before they send it: the sentence is mostly fine, but one part is wrong and you must point to exactly that part. Exams split a sentence into segments (A, B, C, D) and ask which one contains the mistake — or whether there is 'no error'. The skill is knowing the usual places errors hide.
learn.coreIdea: You read each part of the sentence and test it against the common error categories: subject–verb agreement, wrong tense, wrong preposition, wrong pronoun, article misuse, wrong word (adjective vs adverb), and redundancy. The error is almost always in ONE segment; the rest are correct. If every part passes every check, the answer is 'No error'.
learn.howItWorks: Use a fixed error checklist on each segment: (1) does the verb agree with the real subject? (2) is the tense consistent with the rest of the sentence? (3) is the preposition the correct fixed one? (4) does each pronoun match its noun in number and case? (5) is 'a/an/the' used correctly (by sound and specificity)? (6) is an adverb used where a verb is modified (not an adjective)? (7) is any word redundant (e.g. 'return back', 'more better')? The first segment that fails a check is the error; if none fail, choose 'No error'.

rules.name: Agreement error
rules.explanation: Watch subjects split by phrases.
rules.example: 'One of the boys ARE' → should be 'is'.

rules.name: Tense error
rules.explanation: All verbs should fit the timeline.

rules.name: Preposition error
rules.explanation: Compare to the standard combination.

rules.name: Pronoun error
rules.explanation: Match to the word it replaces.

rules.name: Redundancy
rules.explanation: Remove doubled meaning.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Agreement error
workedTypes.idea: Find mismatched verb. What makes it different: subject hidden by a phrase.
workedTypes.problem: Spot the error: (A) One of the students / (B) who came late / (C) were punished / (D) No error.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Preposition error
workedTypes.idea: Wrong fixed pair. What makes it different: extra/incorrect preposition.
workedTypes.problem: Spot the error: (A) The manager / (B) discussed about / (C) the new policy / (D) No error.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Pronoun error
workedTypes.idea: Mismatch in number. What makes it different: 'each/every' is singular.
workedTypes.problem: Spot the error: (A) Every boy / (B) must bring / (C) their own book / (D) No error.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Redundancy error
workedTypes.idea: Doubled meaning. What makes it different: an unnecessary word.
workedTypes.problem: Spot the error: (A) He asked me / (B) to return back / (C) the borrowed book / (D) No error.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — No error
workedTypes.idea: Everything checks out. What makes it different: no segment fails.
workedTypes.problem: Spot the error: (A) She has been working / (B) in this office / (C) since 2018 / (D) No error.

traps: Marking a segment 'wrong' because it sounds unusual, not because a rule is broken.
Missing 'One of / Each / Every' as singular subjects.
Overlooking redundant words like 'return back', 'more better', 'cousin brother'.
Forgetting that 'discuss', 'order', 'request' take no preposition.
Never choosing 'No error' even when the sentence is correct.
learn.memoryHook: Test each segment against the checklist: Agreement, Tense, Preposition, Pronoun, Article, Adjective/Adverb, Redundancy. The first segment that fails is your answer; if none fail, pick 'No error'.

crack.oneLine: Run each segment through a fixed error checklist (agreement, tense, preposition, pronoun, article, adjective/adverb, redundancy) — the first segment that breaks a rule is the error, and if none do, the answer is 'No error'.

strategies.name: Find the real subject
strategies.method: Strip 'of/who/which' phrases, then check the verb.

strategies.name: Preposition scan
strategies.method: Flag verbs that take no preposition (discuss, order, request).

strategies.name: Singular quantifiers
strategies.method: 'each/every/one of' → singular verb & pronoun.

strategies.name: Redundancy hunt
strategies.method: Look for doubled meaning.

crack.whyItWorks: Exam sentences are engineered so that exactly one segment violates a standard rule. A disciplined checklist guarantees you test the same likely error points every time, instead of relying on ear alone.
bestFor: Segment-based 'spot the error' and 'no error' questions.
Sentences designed around a single grammar rule.
notFor: Style/register questions with subjective 'errors'.
Regional usage that differs from standard exam English.
crack.practicePattern: Every error-spotting question: check each segment against the checklist and mark the first rule-breaker (or 'No error').

topics.description: Rewrite faulty sentences into grammatically correct ones.

learn.realLife: Sentence correction is the editor's job: a phrase in a sentence is underlined, and you choose the best replacement from a few options (one of which is 'no change needed'). It's the same rulebook as error spotting, but instead of just FINDING the mistake you must pick the correct REWRITE — clear, grammatical, and not wordy.
learn.coreIdea: An underlined part is offered with alternatives. You reject options that break a grammar rule (agreement, tense, preposition, pronoun, parallelism, modifier placement) AND options that are correct but clumsy or wordy. The best answer is grammatical, keeps the intended meaning, and is concise. If the original is already best, choose 'No correction required'.
learn.howItWorks: Strategy: (1) spot what rule the underlined part tests; (2) eliminate options that break it; (3) among the survivors, prefer the clearest and most concise wording that preserves meaning; (4) plug your choice back in and read the whole sentence. Watch for: subject–verb agreement, correct tense/sequence, fixed prepositions, pronoun agreement, parallel structure, misplaced/dangling modifiers, and redundancy. The correct option is often shorter and more direct.

rules.name: Eliminate rule-breakers
rules.explanation: Remove agreement/tense/preposition faults first.

rules.name: Prefer concise
rules.explanation: Avoid wordy or redundant phrasing.

rules.name: Keep meaning
rules.explanation: A grammatical option that alters meaning is wrong.
rules.example: Don't flip who did the action.

rules.name: Fix modifiers
rules.rule: modifier next to what it describes
rules.explanation: Avoid dangling/misplaced modifiers.

rules.name: Plug and read
rules.explanation: Confirm the whole sentence flows.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Verb form / tense
workedTypes.idea: Fix the verb. What makes it different: wrong participle or sequence.
workedTypes.problem: Correct the underlined part: 'He has went to the market.'

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Subject–verb agreement
workedTypes.idea: Match number. What makes it different: distracting phrase.
workedTypes.problem: Correct: 'The quality of the products are excellent.'

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Parallelism
workedTypes.idea: Match forms. What makes it different: mixed list forms.
workedTypes.problem: Correct: 'She is intelligent, hardworking and she has creativity.'

steps.action: The list mixes adjectives with a clause.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Conciseness / redundancy
workedTypes.idea: Trim wordiness. What makes it different: correct but bloated.
workedTypes.problem: Correct: 'In spite of the fact that it rained, we went out.'

steps.action: 'In spite of the fact that' is wordy.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — No correction required
workedTypes.idea: Original is best. What makes it different: no option improves it.
workedTypes.problem: Correct: 'Neither of the answers is correct.'

traps: Picking a grammatical option that changes the intended meaning.
Choosing a wordy option when a concise one is available.
Fixing one error but introducing another in the rewrite.
Ignoring 'No correction required' when the original is already correct.
Not re-reading the full sentence after substituting the option.
learn.memoryHook: Eliminate rule-breakers, then choose the clearest, shortest option that keeps the meaning — and re-read the whole sentence before locking it in.

crack.oneLine: Reject options that break a grammar rule or change the meaning, then among the correct ones pick the clearest and most concise wording — and if nothing beats the original, choose 'No correction required'.

strategies.name: Error-first elimination
strategies.method: Cross out options with agreement/tense/preposition faults.

strategies.name: Concise wins
strategies.method: Prefer the shorter, direct option.

strategies.name: Meaning guard
strategies.method: Reject options that shift the sense.

strategies.name: Plug and read
strategies.method: Reinsert and read the whole sentence.

signalGuide.signal: 'due to the fact that / in spite of the fact that'

crack.whyItWorks: The correct rewrite must satisfy every grammar rule AND communicate the same idea efficiently. Eliminating rule-breakers first, then favouring concise meaning-preserving wording, reliably isolates the single best option.
bestFor: Underlined-part multiple-choice correction questions.
Items with a clear single rule plus a concise best answer.
notFor: Highly stylistic choices with several defensible answers.
Questions where meaning is genuinely ambiguous.
crack.practicePattern: Every sentence-correction question: eliminate rule-breakers, keep the meaning, choose the most concise correct option, and re-read.

groups.description: Grow your word power — meanings, synonyms, idioms and phrasal verbs.

topics.description: Build word power — synonyms, antonyms, one-word substitution and exact meanings.

learn.realLife: A strong vocabulary is like having the right tool for every job — you understand what you read faster, write more precisely, and score easily on synonym, antonym and 'one-word' questions. You don't need to memorise a dictionary; you need a smart method: learn word ROOTS, use context, and recognise the common question shapes.
learn.coreIdea: Vocabulary questions come in a few fixed shapes: SYNONYM (word with the closest meaning), ANTONYM (word with the opposite meaning), ONE-WORD SUBSTITUTION (a single word for a phrase, e.g. 'a person who eats everything' = omnivore), and MEANING-IN-CONTEXT (what a word means in a given sentence). The fastest way in is to break a word into its parts — prefix (start), root (core meaning) and suffix (end) — and to use the sentence around it as a clue.
learn.howItWorks: Use three tools. (1) Roots & affixes: 'bene' = good (benefit, benevolent), 'mal' = bad (malice), 'anti' = against, 'un/in/dis' = not. Knowing parts lets you guess unfamiliar words. (2) Context: the surrounding words hint at positive/negative tone and meaning. (3) Elimination: for synonyms/antonyms, remove options that are clearly the wrong tone or part of speech, then choose the closest. For one-word substitution, match the phrase's key idea to the word's root.

rules.name: Synonym
rules.explanation: Pick the option nearest in sense and tone.

rules.name: Antonym
rules.explanation: Pick the option that reverses the sense.

rules.name: One-word substitution
rules.explanation: Match the phrase's core idea to a root.

rules.name: Prefix meaning
rules.explanation: The start flips or colours the meaning.

rules.name: Context clue
rules.explanation: Positive vs negative context narrows choices.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Synonym
workedTypes.idea: Closest meaning. What makes it different: pick nearest, not just related.
workedTypes.problem: Choose the synonym of 'ABANDON': (a) keep (b) desert (c) build (d) enjoy.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Antonym
workedTypes.idea: Opposite meaning. What makes it different: reverse the sense.
workedTypes.problem: Choose the antonym of 'GENEROUS': (a) kind (b) rich (c) stingy (d) tall.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — One-word substitution
workedTypes.idea: Phrase to word. What makes it different: match the key idea.
workedTypes.problem: One word for 'a person who cannot read or write': (a) illiterate (b) educated (c) scholar (d) author.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Meaning in context
workedTypes.idea: Use the sentence. What makes it different: same word, context-set meaning.
workedTypes.problem: In 'The novel was a real page-turner', 'page-turner' means: (a) boring (b) very engaging (c) short (d) old.

steps.action: 'page-turner' = so gripping you keep turning pages.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Root/prefix guess
workedTypes.idea: Break the word. What makes it different: unknown word decoded by parts.
workedTypes.problem: Guess the meaning of 'BENEVOLENT': (a) harmful (b) kind (c) fast (d) noisy.

traps: Choosing a word that is merely related instead of the CLOSEST in meaning.
Ignoring word tone (positive vs negative) when eliminating options.
Forgetting prefixes flip meaning ('in-', 'un-', 'dis-', 'mal-').
Picking a synonym when the question asks for an antonym (read carefully).
Overlooking context in meaning-in-context questions.
learn.memoryHook: Decode the word (prefix + root + suffix), read the tone from context, then eliminate wrong-tone/part-of-speech options and pick the closest match.

crack.oneLine: Break unfamiliar words into prefix + root + suffix, read the tone from context, and use elimination to pick the closest synonym / true antonym / matching one-word substitution.

strategies.name: Root decode
strategies.method: Split into meaningful parts.

strategies.name: Tone match
strategies.method: Positive word → positive answer.

strategies.name: Opposite flip
strategies.method: For antonyms, negate the meaning.

strategies.name: Phrase-to-root
strategies.method: Match the phrase's key idea to a word root.

crack.whyItWorks: Most English words are built from a small set of Latin/Greek roots and affixes, so decoding parts plus reading tone reconstructs meaning even for unfamiliar words — and elimination handles the rest.
bestFor: Synonym, antonym, one-word and meaning-in-context questions.
Words built on recognisable roots and affixes.
notFor: Highly idiomatic or slang words with no transparent parts.
Fine shades between two very close synonyms.
crack.practicePattern: Every vocabulary question: decode the word, read the tone, eliminate wrong options, pick the closest match.

topics.description: Understand common idioms and their real meanings.

videos.label: Idioms & Phrases asked in SSC exams (200+)
videos.note: Previous-year idioms with meanings & memory tricks

videos.label: 50 most important idioms & phrases in 10 min

learn.realLife: When someone says 'it's a piece of cake', they don't mean dessert — they mean it's very easy. Idioms are fixed expressions whose meaning is NOT the literal sum of their words. English is full of them, and exams test whether you know the real, figurative meaning. The trick is that you can't translate word-by-word; you learn each idiom as a single unit.
learn.coreIdea: An idiom is a set phrase with a figurative meaning ('spill the beans' = reveal a secret). Exam questions give you an idiom and ask for its meaning, or give a situation and ask which idiom fits. Because the meaning is not literal, you either recognise the idiom or use context and the tone/imagery of the words to make a smart guess. Grouping idioms by theme (money, time, difficulty, secrecy) makes them easier to recall.
learn.howItWorks: Approach: (1) Recognise it as an idiom — the literal reading makes little sense in context. (2) Recall the stored meaning, or infer from imagery ('under the weather' → feeling low → unwell; 'once in a blue moon' → rare event). (3) Match tone: positive idioms map to positive answers. (4) Eliminate literal-trap options — the wrong choices often restate the words literally. Learn high-frequency idioms in themed clusters for fast recall.

rules.name: Figurative, not literal
rules.explanation: Treat the phrase as one unit.

rules.name: Imagery clue
rules.explanation: The mental image hints at meaning.

rules.name: Tone match
rules.explanation: Match emotional colour.

rules.name: Reject literal trap
rules.explanation: Literal restatement is usually wrong.

rules.name: Theme grouping
rules.explanation: Money/time/difficulty idioms learned together.
rules.example: 'cost an arm and a leg' = expensive.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Meaning of an idiom
workedTypes.idea: Recall the sense. What makes it different: figurative meaning.
workedTypes.problem: 'A piece of cake' means: (a) a dessert (b) very easy (c) very costly (d) a small piece.

steps.action: It's an idiom, so ignore the literal cake.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Idiom for a situation
workedTypes.idea: Match phrase to scene. What makes it different: pick the fitting idiom.
workedTypes.problem: Which idiom fits 'to reveal a secret'? (a) break the ice (b) spill the beans (c) hit the sack (d) beat about the bush.

steps.action: The scene is revealing a secret.

steps.why: It means to reveal a secret.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Imagery inference
workedTypes.idea: Use the picture. What makes it different: infer from the image.
workedTypes.problem: 'Under the weather' most likely means: (a) outdoors (b) unwell (c) very busy (d) angry.

steps.action: Picture being weighed down by weather.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Reject the literal trap
workedTypes.idea: Avoid word-for-word. What makes it different: literal option is a trap.
workedTypes.problem: 'To beat about the bush' means: (a) to hit plants (b) to avoid the main point (c) to go hiking (d) to be angry.

steps.action: It means 'to avoid the main point'.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Tone match
workedTypes.idea: Positive/negative. What makes it different: emotional colour picks the answer.
workedTypes.problem: 'On cloud nine' means: (a) confused (b) extremely happy (c) very tired (d) lost.

steps.action: The image is light and high — positive.

traps: Reading the idiom literally instead of figuratively.
Falling for the literal-restatement trap option.
Confusing similar-sounding idioms ('break the ice' vs 'spill the beans').
Ignoring the tone (positive vs negative) of the idiom.
Guessing without using the imagery or context for a clue.
learn.memoryHook: Treat the idiom as ONE unit: never translate word-for-word. Picture the words, match the tone, and reject the literal-trap option.

crack.oneLine: Idioms mean something other than their literal words, so treat each as a single unit — recall the stored meaning or infer it from imagery and tone, and always reject the literal-restatement trap.

strategies.name: One-unit rule
strategies.method: Never translate word-by-word.

strategies.name: Imagery guess
strategies.method: Picture the words, infer the sense.

strategies.name: Tone filter
strategies.method: Match positive/negative colour.

strategies.name: Literal-trap reject
strategies.method: Drop the word-for-word option.

signalGuide.signal: an option that restates the words

crack.whyItWorks: Idioms are lexicalised units — their meaning is fixed by convention, not grammar — so recognising them as wholes (helped by imagery and tone) is far more reliable than parsing the individual words.
bestFor: Idiom-meaning and situation-to-idiom questions.
Common, high-frequency idioms.
notFor: Very rare or regional idioms you have never seen.
Phrases that are also used literally in some contexts.
crack.practicePattern: Every idiom question: read it as one unit, use imagery + tone, and reject the literal option.

topics.description: Common verb + preposition combos (like 'give up') and their meanings.

learn.realLife: We say 'give up smoking', 'put off the meeting', 'run out of milk' — everyday English is packed with phrasal verbs: a verb plus a small word (up, off, out, in, on) that together take on a NEW meaning. 'Give up' isn't about giving; it means to quit. Exams test whether you know these combined meanings, because you can't work them out from the verb alone.
learn.coreIdea: A phrasal verb = verb + particle (a preposition or adverb) that forms a single meaning unit ('look after' = take care of). The same verb changes meaning completely with a different particle: 'put off' = postpone, 'put up with' = tolerate, 'put on' = wear. Questions ask for the meaning of a phrasal verb, or which phrasal verb fits a sentence. Learn them by the base verb and its particles, and use context to choose.
learn.howItWorks: Two ideas help. (1) The PARTICLE colours the meaning: 'up' often = complete/increase (finish up, speed up), 'off' = away/cancel (call off), 'out' = finish/discover (find out, run out), 'down' = reduce/record (cut down, write down). (2) CONTEXT decides which meaning of a multi-sense verb applies ('put on weight' vs 'put on a coat'). Group phrasal verbs by base verb (get, put, take, look, break…) and memorise the common ones with a one-line meaning.

rules.name: Verb + particle = new meaning
rules.explanation: The unit means more than the verb.

rules.name: Particle colours sense
rules.explanation: The small word shifts meaning.

rules.name: Same verb, many meanings
rules.rule: put off / put on / put up with
rules.explanation: Particle changes everything.

rules.name: Context picks the sense
rules.explanation: Multi-sense phrasal verbs need context.

rules.name: Group by base verb
rules.explanation: Learn families together.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Meaning of a phrasal verb
workedTypes.idea: Recall the unit. What makes it different: combined meaning.
workedTypes.problem: 'Give up' means: (a) to lift (b) to quit (c) to donate (d) to raise.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Phrasal verb for a sentence
workedTypes.idea: Fit the blank. What makes it different: choose the right particle.
workedTypes.problem: 'The meeting was ___ due to rain.' (a) put on (b) put off (c) put up (d) put in.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Particle sense
workedTypes.idea: Use the particle. What makes it different: 'out' = discover/finish.
workedTypes.problem: 'We ran ___ of petrol.' (a) out (b) in (c) up (d) off.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Same verb, right particle
workedTypes.idea: Pick the meaning. What makes it different: verb has many particles.
workedTypes.problem: 'I can't ___ his rudeness anymore.' (a) put on (b) put off (c) put up with (d) put in.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Context sense
workedTypes.idea: Context decides. What makes it different: one phrasal verb, two senses.
workedTypes.problem: In 'The plane took off on time', 'took off' means: (a) removed (b) departed (c) copied (d) reduced.

traps: Reading a phrasal verb literally ('give up' as physically giving).
Choosing the wrong particle ('put off' vs 'put up with').
Ignoring context when a phrasal verb has two meanings ('take off').
Confusing similar verbs ('bring up' vs 'bring about').
Forgetting that the particle changes the whole meaning.
learn.memoryHook: Learn phrasal verbs as whole units, grouped by base verb. Let the particle hint at the sense (up=complete, off=cancel, out=finish/discover) and let context settle multi-meaning ones.

crack.oneLine: A phrasal verb (verb + particle) has a single meaning that isn't the verb's literal sense — learn them as units grouped by base verb, use the particle as a clue, and let context pick between multiple meanings.

strategies.name: Unit recall
strategies.method: Treat verb + particle as one word.

strategies.name: Particle clue
strategies.method: up=complete, off=cancel, out=finish/discover, down=reduce.

strategies.name: Verb family
strategies.method: Group by base verb (put/get/take/look).

strategies.name: Context decide
strategies.method: Use the sentence for multi-sense verbs.

signalGuide.use: put off / call off / put up with.

crack.whyItWorks: Phrasal verbs are fixed lexical units whose meaning is conventional, and the particle carries a consistent nuance often enough to guide guesses — so unit-recall plus particle sense plus context handles almost all questions.
bestFor: Meaning-of-phrasal-verb and fill-the-particle questions.
Common phrasal verbs grouped by base verb.
notFor: Rare or highly idiomatic phrasal verbs.
Sentences where two phrasal verbs both fit grammatically.
crack.practicePattern: Every phrasal-verb question: recall the unit, use the particle as a clue, and let context pick the sense.

groups.description: Complete, join and tighten sentences correctly.

topics.description: Pick the word or phrase that best fills the blank.

videos.label: Sentence Completion — tips & tricks (TCS NQT)
videos.note: Placement-focused: clue words, elimination, two-blank method

learn.realLife: Sentence completion is like filling a missing piece in a jigsaw: the sentence around the blank already tells you the shape of the word that fits. You're given a sentence with one or two blanks and must choose the option that makes it grammatically correct AND logically sensible. The winning skill is reading the CLUE WORDS that signal what kind of word belongs.
learn.coreIdea: You pick the word/phrase that best fits a blank. The sentence gives clues: signal words like 'because', 'so', 'therefore' show cause/effect (the blank continues the same idea); contrast words like 'but', 'although', 'however', 'despite' show the blank flips direction; and the tone (positive/negative) narrows the choice. For two-blank questions, both must fit together. Grammar (part of speech, tense) plus logic (meaning + tone) decide the answer.
learn.howItWorks: Method: (1) Read the whole sentence, noting the blank's role. (2) Find the SIGNAL word: 'and/so/because/therefore' → continue the same tone; 'but/although/however/yet/despite' → opposite tone. (3) Predict the meaning of the missing word BEFORE looking at options. (4) Check grammar — the option must be the right part of speech and tense. (5) Eliminate options that clash in tone or grammar, then plug the survivor back and re-read. For two blanks, test the pair together and reject any pair where either blank fails.

rules.name: Continuation signal
rules.rule: and / so / because / therefore → same tone
rules.explanation: The blank continues the idea.

rules.name: Contrast signal
rules.rule: but / although / however / despite → opposite tone
rules.explanation: The blank flips the direction.

rules.name: Predict then match
rules.rule: guess the word, then find it
rules.explanation: Predicting avoids option traps.
rules.example: Expect a negative word after 'but happy... '

rules.name: Grammar fit
rules.explanation: The option must fit grammatically.
rules.example: Need a noun, not a verb.

rules.name: Two-blank test
rules.explanation: Reject a pair if either fails.
rules.example: Test the pair as a unit.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Cause/continuation
workedTypes.idea: Same tone. What makes it different: 'so/because' continues the idea.
workedTypes.problem: 'She was very tired, so she ___ early.' (a) woke (b) slept (c) ran (d) ate.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Contrast
workedTypes.idea: Opposite tone. What makes it different: 'but/although' flips it.
workedTypes.problem: 'He is rich but ___.' (a) wealthy (b) generous (c) unhappy (d) successful.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Vocabulary fit
workedTypes.idea: Right meaning. What makes it different: precise word choice.
workedTypes.problem: 'The scientist made an important ___ that changed medicine.' (a) mistake (b) discovery (c) noise (d) delay.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Grammar fit
workedTypes.idea: Right form. What makes it different: part of speech/tense matters.
workedTypes.problem: 'The children were ___ in the garden.' (a) play (b) plays (c) playing (d) played.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Two blanks
workedTypes.idea: Pair fit. What makes it different: both must work together.
workedTypes.problem: '___ it rained heavily, the match ___.' (a) Because / continued (b) Although / continued (c) So / stopped (d) If / stopped.

steps.action: Heavy rain would normally stop a match, so continuing is a surprise.
steps.why: A surprising result signals contrast, not cause-effect.

steps.action: 'Although ... continued' fits: rain heavy, yet the match went on.
steps.why: Both blanks work together for the contrast.

traps: Ignoring the signal word ('but' vs 'so') and choosing the wrong tone.
Not predicting the word first, so option traps mislead you.
Choosing a word that fits meaning but is the wrong part of speech.
In two-blank questions, checking only one blank.
Not re-reading the full sentence after inserting the choice.
learn.memoryHook: Find the signal word: continuation (and/so/because) keeps the tone; contrast (but/although/however) flips it. Predict the word, check grammar, then plug and re-read.

crack.oneLine: Read the signal word to decide tone (continuation vs contrast), predict the missing word before looking, then pick the option that fits both meaning and grammar — testing both blanks together in two-blank questions.

strategies.name: Signal-word tone
strategies.method: and/so/because → same; but/although/however → opposite.

strategies.name: Predict first
strategies.method: Guess the word before reading options.

strategies.name: Grammar filter
strategies.method: Reject wrong part of speech/tense.

strategies.name: Pair test
strategies.method: Test both blanks as a unit.

signalGuide.use: Use the -ing or correct verb form.

crack.whyItWorks: A well-formed sentence constrains the blank in both meaning and grammar, and the connector fixes the logical direction — so reading the signal and predicting the word narrows the choice to essentially one option.
bestFor: Single- and double-blank fill-in questions with clear connectors.
Sentences with a definite tone or logical link.
notFor: Sentences with several equally acceptable words.
Subtle style choices without a clear signal.
crack.practicePattern: Every completion question: read the signal, predict the word, check grammar, and plug the best fit back in.

topics.description: Fill missing letters to complete the correct word.

learn.realLife: Word completion is the puzzle you play whenever autocorrect guesses your word from a few letters, or when you solve a crossword with some letters filled in. You're shown a word with blanks (like 'B _ _ K') and must supply the missing letters to form a valid, correctly-spelled word. It rewards spelling sense and pattern recognition.
learn.coreIdea: Some letters of a word are hidden and you fill them in — sometimes to make ANY valid word, sometimes a word matching a given clue/meaning. You use the visible letters, the word length, and common spelling patterns (prefixes, suffixes, digraphs like 'ch/sh/th', vowel placement) to reconstruct it. Every English word needs vowels in sensible positions, so vowel placement is often the key.
learn.howItWorks: Steps: (1) Note the fixed letters and the number of blanks. (2) Recall that most syllables need a vowel (a, e, i, o, u) — place vowels first. (3) Use common patterns: words often start with a consonant + vowel, use suffixes ('-ing', '-tion', '-ed', '-ly'), or prefixes ('un-', 're-', 'in-'). (4) If a clue/meaning is given, brainstorm words of that length matching it. (5) Verify: the completed word must be a real, correctly-spelled English word. Try candidates and keep the one that reads correctly.

rules.name: Vowel-first
rules.explanation: Fill vowels into blanks first.

rules.name: Common suffix
rules.explanation: Endings fix the last letters.

rules.name: Common prefix
rules.explanation: Starts fix the first letters.

rules.name: Digraph patterns
rules.explanation: Two letters, one sound.

rules.name: Length + clue
rules.explanation: Fit the clue into the blanks.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Fill the vowels
workedTypes.idea: Vowel placement. What makes it different: consonants given.
workedTypes.problem: Complete the word: 'B _ _ K' (a common object you read).

steps.action: Consonants B and K; need vowels between.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Suffix completion
workedTypes.idea: Fix the ending. What makes it different: common suffix.
workedTypes.problem: Complete: 'ACT _ _ N' (a thing done).

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Prefix completion
workedTypes.idea: Fix the start. What makes it different: common prefix.
workedTypes.problem: Complete: '_ _ HAPPY' (not happy).

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Digraph completion
workedTypes.idea: Two-letter sound. What makes it different: ch/sh/th pattern.
workedTypes.problem: Complete: '_ _ IP' (a large boat).

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Clue + length
workedTypes.idea: Match meaning. What makes it different: use the clue and count.
workedTypes.problem: Complete the 5-letter fruit: 'A _ _ L E'.

traps: Forming a string that isn't a real word — always verify spelling.
Forgetting most syllables need a vowel.
Ignoring the clue or the required word length.
Overlooking double letters (book, apple, letter).
Missing common prefixes/suffixes that fix several letters at once.
learn.memoryHook: Place vowels first, then test common prefixes/suffixes and digraphs, and always confirm the result is a real, correctly-spelled word that matches the clue.

crack.oneLine: Fill blanks by placing vowels first, then applying common prefixes, suffixes and digraphs — always checking the result is a real word of the right length that matches any given clue.

strategies.name: Vowel-first
strategies.method: Drop vowels into blanks to form syllables.

strategies.name: Affix test
strategies.method: Try un-/re-/in- and -ing/-tion/-ed/-ly.

strategies.name: Digraph fill
strategies.method: Use ch/sh/th/ck/ph for paired blanks.

strategies.name: Clue + count
strategies.method: Brainstorm words of that length matching the clue.

signalGuide.signal: two adjacent blanks at the start

signalGuide.use: Match a word to the count.

crack.whyItWorks: English spelling follows strong regularities — vowels per syllable, a small set of frequent affixes, and fixed digraphs — so applying these patterns reconstructs the intended word far faster than random guessing.
bestFor: Missing-letter and jumbled-letter word puzzles.
Words built on common affixes and patterns.
notFor: Irregularly spelled or loan words.
Blanks with several valid completions and no clue.
crack.practicePattern: Every word-completion question: vowels first, test affixes/digraphs, match the clue, and verify a real word.

topics.description: Combine two sentences correctly using connectors.

learn.realLife: Good writing links ideas smoothly instead of leaving choppy short sentences. Sentence joining tests exactly that: you're given two sentences and must combine them into one correct sentence using the right connector — 'and', 'but', 'so', 'because', 'although', 'who', 'which', etc. Choose the connector that matches the logical RELATIONSHIP between the two ideas.
learn.coreIdea: You merge two sentences into one. First decide the relationship: addition (and), contrast (but/although/however), cause–effect (because/so/since), time (when/after/before), condition (if/unless), or description (who/which/that). Then pick the connector that expresses it, and adjust the sentence so it stays grammatical (no repetition, correct punctuation). The meaning must not change.
learn.howItWorks: Relationship → connector map: ADDITION → and, as well as, not only...but also; CONTRAST → but, yet, although, though, however, despite; CAUSE/EFFECT → because, since, as, so, therefore; TIME → when, while, after, before, as soon as; CONDITION → if, unless, provided that; RELATIVE (describing a noun) → who (people), which (things), that. Use subordinating connectors (because, although, when) to make one idea depend on the other, and coordinating ones (and, but, so) to join equals. Remove repeated words and keep one clear subject–verb structure.

rules.name: Addition
rules.rule: and / as well as / not only...but also
rules.explanation: Two ideas add up.

rules.name: Contrast
rules.explanation: Ideas oppose each other.

rules.name: Cause–effect
rules.explanation: One idea causes the other.

rules.name: Time
rules.rule: when / while / after / before / as soon as
rules.explanation: One event relative to another.

rules.name: Relative clause
rules.explanation: Describe a noun with a clause.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Contrast
workedTypes.idea: Opposition. What makes it different: 'although/but' links opposite ideas.
workedTypes.problem: Join: 'He is poor. He is honest.'

steps.action: 'Although he is poor, he is honest.'

workedTypes.answer: 'Although he is poor, he is honest.'

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Cause/effect
workedTypes.idea: Reason. What makes it different: 'because/so' links cause and result.
workedTypes.problem: Join: 'It was raining. We stayed at home.'

steps.action: 'We stayed at home because it was raining.'

workedTypes.answer: 'We stayed at home because it was raining.'

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Relative clause
workedTypes.idea: Describe a noun. What makes it different: 'who/which' adds detail.
workedTypes.problem: Join: 'This is the book. It won the prize.'

steps.action: 'This is the book which won the prize.'

workedTypes.answer: 'This is the book which won the prize.'

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Time
workedTypes.idea: Sequence. What makes it different: 'when/after' orders events.
workedTypes.problem: Join: 'He finished his work. He went out.'

steps.action: 'After he finished his work, he went out.'

workedTypes.answer: 'After he finished his work, he went out.'

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Condition
workedTypes.idea: If-then. What makes it different: 'if/unless' sets a condition.
workedTypes.problem: Join: 'Work hard. You will pass.'

steps.action: 'If you work hard, you will pass.'

workedTypes.answer: 'If you work hard, you will pass.'

traps: Choosing a connector that changes the intended meaning.
Using two connectors for the same link ('Although ... but ...').
Leaving repeated words instead of merging cleanly.
Mismatching who/which (who for people, which for things).
Wrong punctuation — missing the comma after an opening clause.
learn.memoryHook: Name the relationship first (add / contrast / cause / time / condition / describe), then pick the one connector that fits — and never use two connectors for one link.

crack.oneLine: Identify the logical relationship between the two sentences (addition, contrast, cause, time, condition, or description) and join them with the single connector that expresses it — without repeating words or changing the meaning.

strategies.name: Relationship map
strategies.method: Match the link to its connector.

strategies.name: One-connector rule
strategies.method: Use only ONE connector per link.

strategies.name: Who/which pick
strategies.method: who = people, which = things.

strategies.name: Merge & trim
strategies.method: Remove repeated subject/words.

drill.question: describe a thing → who or which?

crack.whyItWorks: Two sentences relate in a limited number of logical ways, and English provides a dedicated connector for each. Naming the relationship first turns the choice into a simple lookup, while the one-connector and merge rules keep the result grammatical.
bestFor: Combine-the-sentences and choose-the-connector questions.
Pairs with a clear logical relationship.
notFor: Pairs that could plausibly link in more than one way.
Complex multi-clause combinations with nuance.
crack.practicePattern: Every joining question: name the relationship, pick the single matching connector, merge cleanly, and re-read.

topics.description: Shorten a long sentence while keeping its meaning.

learn.realLife: Condensation is the skill behind a good headline or a tight tweet: say the same thing in fewer words. Exams give a long, wordy sentence and ask you to pick the shortest version that keeps the FULL meaning. It's the opposite of padding — you strip filler while protecting the core idea.
learn.coreIdea: You shorten a sentence without losing meaning or grammar. Cut redundant phrases ('due to the fact that' → 'because'), replace long phrases with single words ('a person who steals' → 'thief'), remove needless repetition, and turn clauses into phrases where possible. The best answer is the shortest option that is still complete, correct, and carries the same meaning as the original.
learn.howItWorks: Techniques: (1) Replace wordy connectors — 'due to the fact that / in spite of the fact that' → 'because / although'. (2) Use one-word substitutes for phrases ('one who cannot read' → 'illiterate'). (3) Delete redundancy ('true fact', 'final conclusion', 'return back'). (4) Reduce a relative clause to a phrase ('the man who was tired' → 'the tired man'). (5) Prefer active voice, which is usually shorter. Then check: does the condensed version keep EVERY essential idea and stay grammatical? If it drops meaning, it's wrong even if shortest.

rules.name: Trim wordy connectors
rules.explanation: Replace phrases with one word.

rules.name: One-word substitution
rules.explanation: Compress a description.

rules.name: Cut redundancy
rules.explanation: Delete repeated ideas.

rules.name: Reduce clauses
rules.explanation: Turn 'who is X' into 'X'.
rules.example: 'the man who is tall' → 'the tall man'.

rules.name: Keep the meaning
rules.explanation: Never drop an essential idea.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Wordy connector
workedTypes.idea: Replace the phrase. What makes it different: bloated linker.
workedTypes.problem: Condense: 'Due to the fact that he was ill, he stayed home.'

steps.action: 'Because he was ill, he stayed home.'

workedTypes.answer: 'Because he was ill, he stayed home.'

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — One-word substitution
workedTypes.idea: Compress a phrase. What makes it different: description → one word.
workedTypes.problem: Condense: 'A person who writes books is respected.'

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Cut redundancy
workedTypes.idea: Remove doubling. What makes it different: repeated meaning.
workedTypes.problem: Condense: 'He returned back the book that he had borrowed.'

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Reduce a clause
workedTypes.idea: Clause to phrase. What makes it different: 'who is' can be dropped.
workedTypes.problem: Condense: 'The boy who was standing there is my brother.'

steps.action: 'The boy standing there is my brother.'

workedTypes.answer: 'The boy standing there is my brother.'

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Choose shortest correct
workedTypes.idea: Best option. What makes it different: shortest that keeps meaning.
workedTypes.problem: Choose the best condensed form of 'In spite of the fact that it was late, they continued to keep on working.'

steps.action: 'In spite of the fact that' → 'Although'; 'continued to keep on' → 'kept'.
'Although it was late, they kept working.'

workedTypes.answer: 'Although it was late, they kept working.'

traps: Cutting so much that essential meaning is lost.
Keeping wordy connectors instead of one-word replacements.
Leaving redundant words ('true fact', 'return back').
Introducing a grammar error while shortening.
Choosing a short option that changes the meaning.
learn.memoryHook: Cut filler and redundancy, swap phrases for single words, reduce clauses to phrases — but keep every essential idea. Shortest AND complete wins.

crack.oneLine: Pick the shortest version that still says everything: replace wordy connectors and phrases with single words, delete redundancy, reduce clauses to phrases, and reject any option that loses meaning or breaks grammar.

strategies.name: Connector swap
strategies.method: 'due to/in spite of the fact that' → 'because/although'.

strategies.name: Phrase → word
strategies.method: Use a one-word substitute.

strategies.name: Redundancy cut
strategies.method: Delete doubled meaning.

strategies.name: Clause reduce
strategies.method: Turn 'who is X' into 'X'.

signalGuide.signal: 'due to the fact that / in spite of the fact that'

drill.question: Shortest option always wins? (T/F)
drill.answer: Only if it keeps the meaning

crack.whyItWorks: Wordiness comes from a few repeatable sources — bloated connectors, describable-in-one-word phrases, redundancy and heavy clauses. Targeting these while guarding the core meaning reliably yields the shortest correct sentence.
bestFor: Choose-the-condensed-form and rewrite-concisely questions.
Sentences padded with wordy phrases or redundancy.
notFor: Sentences where shortening removes a necessary nuance.
Legal/technical text where every word is required.
crack.practicePattern: Every condensation question: trim connectors, substitute phrases, cut redundancy, reduce clauses, then pick the shortest option that keeps all meaning.

groups.description: Read passages closely, reorder jumbled lines, and analyse arguments.

topics.description: Read a passage and answer questions on meaning, tone and detail.

videos.label: RC strategy — reading order & method (Rodha)
videos.note: Question-first vs passage-first, main-idea & elimination technique

learn.realLife: Reading comprehension (RC) is exactly what you do when you read a WhatsApp forward and decide if it's true, or skim a rental agreement to find the one clause that matters. You're given a passage and questions, and every answer is hidden somewhere in the text — you never use outside knowledge. The skill is not reading fast; it's reading for what the questions actually ask.
learn.coreIdea: You read a short passage and answer questions about it: its main idea, a specific detail, the meaning of a word in context, the author's tone/attitude, or what can be inferred. The golden rule: the correct answer is supported by the passage, and the wrong ones are either not stated, too extreme, or twist a detail. You are a detective matching options to evidence, not a person sharing an opinion.
learn.howItWorks: A reliable method: (1) Skim the passage once for the gist — what is it about and what is the author's stance? (2) Read the question and identify its type (main idea / detail / vocabulary / tone / inference). (3) For detail and vocab questions, go BACK to the exact line — don't rely on memory. (4) Predict the answer in your own words before reading options. (5) Eliminate: cross out options that add new facts, use absolute words (always, never, only) the passage didn't, or contradict the text. The survivor is your answer.

rules.name: Answer is in the text
rules.rule: Every answer must be provable from the passage — never from what you already know.
rules.explanation: Even if an option is factually true in real life, it is wrong unless the passage says it.
rules.example: If the passage never mentions cost, 'it is expensive' cannot be the answer.

rules.name: Main idea = whole, not part
rules.rule: The main idea covers the entire passage, not just one paragraph.
rules.explanation: Options that are true but only cover one detail are 'too narrow' and wrong.
rules.example: A passage on climate policy → main idea is the policy debate, not one country's rainfall.

rules.name: Beware extreme words
rules.rule: Absolute words (always, never, all, only, must) are usually wrong unless the passage is that strong.
rules.explanation: Authors normally hedge ('often', 'many'); options that overstate are traps.
rules.example: Passage: 'many students struggle' → option 'all students fail' is too extreme.

rules.name: Inference stays close
rules.rule: A valid inference is one small logical step from the text — not a big leap.
rules.explanation: If you need several assumptions to reach an option, it is not a safe inference.
rules.example: 'She packed an umbrella' → safe: she expects rain; unsafe: it will definitely storm.

rules.name: Tone from word choice
rules.rule: The author's tone shows in adjectives and verbs, not just facts.
rules.explanation: Words like 'unfortunately', 'remarkable', 'so-called' reveal approval, criticism or doubt.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Main idea
workedTypes.idea: Asks the central point of the whole passage. What makes it different: right answer summarises everything, not one line.
workedTypes.problem: A passage explains how bees pollinate crops, why bee numbers are falling, and what farmers can do. The main idea is: (a) bees make honey (b) bees are important to farming and are under threat (c) farmers dislike bees (d) honey is expensive.

steps.action: Ask: what ties every paragraph together?
steps.why: Main idea must cover the whole passage.

steps.action: Reject (a) and (d) — only tiny details; (c) — not stated.

workedTypes.answer: (b) bees are important to farming and are under threat.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Detail (fact) question
workedTypes.idea: Asks a specific stated fact. What makes it different: the answer is literally in one line — go find it.
workedTypes.problem: Passage says: 'The library opens at 9 a.m. on weekdays and 10 a.m. on weekends.' Q: When does it open on Sunday?

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Vocabulary in context
workedTypes.idea: Asks the meaning of a word as used here. What makes it different: use the sentence, not the dictionary's first meaning.
workedTypes.problem: 'The plan was sound.' Here 'sound' means: (a) noise (b) sensible (c) loud (d) deep.

steps.action: Replace the word with each option in the sentence.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Inference
workedTypes.idea: Asks what follows logically though not stated. What makes it different: one small step beyond the text.
workedTypes.problem: 'Ravi checked the weather, then cancelled the picnic.' We can infer: (a) Ravi loves picnics (b) the forecast was bad (c) it was sunny (d) Ravi was ill.

steps.action: Link the two facts: checked weather → cancelled.
Bad weather explains it; others aren't supported.

traps: Using your own knowledge instead of the passage — only the text counts.
Picking an option that is true but only covers one detail for a 'main idea' question.
Falling for extreme words (always/never/only) the passage never used.
Answering detail questions from memory instead of returning to the exact line.
learn.memoryHook: Read for the question, answer from the text: right = supported, wrong = unstated, extreme, or twisted.

crack.oneLine: Find the question type, go back to the lines it points to, and pick the option the passage can prove.

strategies.name: Main idea — one-line summary
strategies.method: Summarise the passage in your own 6–8 words, then match.
strategies.howToApply: After skimming, say the gist aloud; pick the option closest to it and widest in scope.
strategies.example: Bees passage → 'bees matter to farming, now under threat' → matches option (b).
strategies.whenFails: Detail questions — those need the exact line, not the gist.

strategies.name: Detail — keyword scan
strategies.method: Grab a keyword from the question and scan the passage for it.
strategies.howToApply: Find the noun/number in the question (e.g. 'Sunday', '1995'), locate it in the text, read that sentence.
strategies.example: Q asks 'Sunday' → scan → 'weekends 10 a.m.' → answer 10 a.m.
strategies.whenWorks: Fact/detail and 'according to the passage' questions.
strategies.whenFails: Inference — the keyword may not appear.

strategies.name: Elimination by red flags
strategies.method: Cross out options that add facts, overstate, or contradict.
strategies.howToApply: Test each option against the text; delete the ones with new info or absolute words.
strategies.example: 'all students fail' when text said 'many struggle' → delete.
strategies.whenFails: When two survivors are both supported — reread for the precise word.

signalGuide.use: Main-idea question — pick the widest supported option.
Detail question — the answer is stated; go find the line.
Inference — take one small logical step from the text.
Vocabulary-in-context — substitute options into the sentence.
Read the adjectives/verbs, not the facts.

drill.question: Right answers must be supported by the passage or by your own knowledge?
For a detail question, do you rely on memory?
drill.answer: No — return to the exact line.

drill.question: A main-idea answer that covers only one paragraph is…?

crack.whyItWorks: RC options are engineered so exactly one is provable and the rest have a flaw. Naming the question type tells you where to look, and elimination by red flags removes the engineered traps.
bestFor: Detail, main-idea and vocabulary questions where evidence is explicit
Timed sections — keyword scanning is fast
notFor: Rushing without skimming the gist first
Inference questions solved by big assumptions
crack.practicePattern: Skim for gist → label the question type → return to the lines → predict → eliminate red flags → confirm.

topics.description: Reorder scrambled sentences into a logical paragraph.

videos.label: How to Solve Para Jumbles — step by step
videos.note: Opening sentence, mandatory pairs & connector logic

learn.realLife: Para jumbles are like the panels of a comic strip handed to you shuffled: each panel makes sense, but only one order tells the story. You're given 4–6 sentences labelled (like P, Q, R, S) in the wrong order and must rebuild the original paragraph. The trick is to follow the thread of ideas — what must come first, what can't come before what.
learn.coreIdea: A paragraph has one logical flow: it introduces something, develops it, and closes. Jumbled sentences hide that flow. You find it using clues — the opening sentence (general, no back-reference), linking words (however, therefore, also), and pronouns/references (this, they, such) that must point to something already mentioned.
learn.howItWorks: Method: (1) Find the OPENING — a sentence that introduces a topic on its own, with no 'this/that/it/however' pointing backwards. (2) Find MANDATORY PAIRS — two sentences that clearly join (a noun then its pronoun; a cause then 'therefore'; a question then its answer). (3) Use TRANSITION words to fix order: 'however/but' flips direction, 'therefore/so' gives a result, 'also/moreover' adds, 'finally' closes. (4) Find the ENDING — often a conclusion or consequence. (5) Assemble and re-read the full paragraph to check it flows.

rules.name: Opening has no back-reference
rules.rule: The first sentence introduces a topic and contains no word pointing to earlier text.
rules.explanation: If a sentence starts with 'This', 'They', 'Such', 'However', something must come before it — so it can't be first.
rules.example: 'Solar power is growing fast.' can open; 'This is why it grew.' cannot.

rules.name: Pronouns need an antecedent
rules.rule: A pronoun (it, they, he, this) must appear AFTER the noun it replaces.
rules.explanation: The sentence naming the noun comes before the sentence using the pronoun.
rules.example: 'Meena joined ISRO.' → 'She led a mission.' (Meena before She).

rules.name: Connectors set direction
rules.rule: However/but = contrast; therefore/so = result; also/moreover = addition; finally = closing.
rules.explanation: These words tell you what the previous sentence must have been.
rules.example: '…However, sales fell.' must follow a sentence about sales rising.

rules.name: Mandatory pairs stick together
rules.rule: Some sentences clearly attach with nothing allowed between them.
rules.explanation: Cause→effect, question→answer, general→example form fixed pairs; lock them, then order the pairs.
rules.example: 'What causes tides? / The moon's pull does.' is a fixed pair.

rules.name: Time & logic flow forward
rules.rule: Events run in time/logical order: setup → development → conclusion.
rules.explanation: Dates, 'first/then/later', and cause-before-effect fix the sequence.
rules.example: 'In 1990 it began.' before 'By 2000 it had spread.'

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Find the opener
workedTypes.idea: Spot the standalone introducer. What makes it different: no back-reference words.
workedTypes.problem: P: However, it failed. Q: A startup launched a food app. R: They ran out of funds. Order the first sentence.

steps.action: P starts with 'However', R with 'They' — both refer back.
Q introduces the startup cleanly → Q is first.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Pronoun pairing
workedTypes.idea: Link noun to its pronoun. What makes it different: pronoun sentence comes after.
workedTypes.problem: A: She won gold. B: Sindhu trained for years. Which comes first?

steps.action: B names Sindhu → B before A.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Connector logic
workedTypes.idea: Use transition words to fix order. What makes it different: the connector implies the prior idea.
workedTypes.problem: X: Therefore, prices dropped. Y: Supply suddenly doubled. Order them.

steps.action: Y (supply doubled) is the cause → Y before X.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Full assembly
workedTypes.idea: Combine opener + pairs + ending. What makes it different: check whole flow at the end.
workedTypes.problem: Order P,Q,R,S — Q opens (topic), S pairs after Q (pronoun), P adds 'also', R closes with 'finally'.

steps.action: Q opens; S follows (its pronoun refers to Q).

traps: Choosing a sentence with 'This/They/However' as the opener.
Ignoring a pronoun whose noun appears in another sentence.
Breaking a mandatory pair by inserting a sentence between them.
Not re-reading the final order to confirm it actually flows.
learn.memoryHook: Opener has no 'back-words'; pronouns follow their nouns; connectors point backward; then read it whole.

crack.oneLine: Lock the opener and the mandatory pairs first — the rest of the order falls into place.

strategies.name: Eliminate impossible openers
strategies.method: Cross out any sentence starting with a back-reference as the first sentence.
strategies.howToApply: Scan starts: this/that/these/they/it/such/however/therefore → cannot be #1. Often leaves one opener.
strategies.example: Only Q lacks back-words → Q is first; wrong options die instantly.
strategies.whenWorks: Almost every para jumble — fastest first move.
strategies.whenFails: Rare cases with two clean openers — then use the pair test.

strategies.name: Mandatory-pair test
strategies.method: Find two sentences that must touch, then check which option keeps them together.
strategies.howToApply: Spot noun→pronoun or cause→'therefore'; eliminate options that split the pair.
strategies.example: Pair BA (Sindhu→She) → reject any sequence with something between B and A.
strategies.whenWorks: MCQ options — kills 2–3 choices at once.
strategies.whenFails: When no obvious pair exists — lean on connectors and time order.

strategies.name: Answer-option shortcut
strategies.method: Test the given order options instead of building from scratch.
strategies.howToApply: Take each option, read it as a paragraph, drop the one that jars at any junction.
strategies.example: Option Q-S-P-R reads smoothly; Q-P-S-R breaks at P → eliminate.

signalGuide.use: Not the opener — needs a prior sentence.
Follows a sentence of the OPPOSITE idea.
Follows the cause; it states the result.
Adds to the previous point — comes after it.

drill.question: Can a sentence beginning 'However' be the opener?
Does the noun or the pronoun come first?
First move on any para jumble?

crack.whyItWorks: A paragraph is a chain of references and transitions. Back-reference words and pronouns pin sentences relative to each other, so fixing the opener and the pairs removes almost all the freedom — the correct order is what's left.
bestFor: Jumbles with clear connectors or pronouns
MCQs where you can test the four given orders
notFor: Guessing the whole order before finding the opener
Ignoring the final flow check
crack.practicePattern: Find opener → lock mandatory pairs → order with connectors/time → read the whole paragraph to confirm.

topics.description: Read, remember and answer from a passage quickly.

learn.realLife: Passage recall is like watching a short news clip and then answering 'who, what, where, when' without replaying it. You read a passage once, and questions test how accurately you remember its facts — names, numbers, order of events, and the single main point. It rewards focused reading and a light mental 'map', not re-reading everything.
learn.coreIdea: You read a passage and answer factual questions mostly from memory (or with a quick glance back). Questions target concrete details: who did what, a figure, a sequence, a definition, and the overall gist. Unlike full comprehension, inference is minimal — it's about capturing and holding the stated facts.
learn.howItWorks: Method: (1) Read actively — as you read, note the KEY nouns (names, places), NUMBERS, and the ORDER of events. (2) Build a one-line gist: what is this passage mainly saying? (3) Anticipate: examiners love the first fact, the last fact, any number, and any contrast. (4) When answering, trust facts you clearly noted; for anything shaky, glance back to the exact spot rather than guessing. (5) Watch for 'swapped detail' traps where an option changes one name/number.

rules.name: Anchor the who/what/number
rules.rule: While reading, lock onto names, roles and figures — these are the most-asked recall points.
rules.explanation: Concrete nouns and numbers are easy to quiz and easy to twist in wrong options.
rules.example: 'Anil, the manager, approved ₹5 lakh' → remember Anil, manager, ₹5 lakh.

rules.name: Keep the sequence
rules.rule: Note the order in which events happen — first, then, finally.
rules.explanation: Recall questions often ask 'what happened after X?' or reverse the order in a trap.
rules.example: 'She resigned, then travelled, then wrote a book.' → order matters.

rules.name: One gist line
rules.rule: Hold a single sentence that captures the whole passage.
rules.explanation: The main-point question is guaranteed; a gist line answers it instantly.
rules.example: Passage on a rescue → gist: 'villagers saved trapped climbers'.

rules.name: Verify shaky facts
rules.rule: If you're not sure, glance back to the exact line instead of guessing.
rules.explanation: A five-second recheck beats losing a mark to a swapped detail.
rules.example: Unsure if it was 1998 or 1989 → look back and confirm.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Who/what detail
workedTypes.idea: Recall a stated fact. What makes it different: it's a direct name/role/number.
workedTypes.problem: Passage: 'Dr. Rao, a botanist, discovered the plant in 2011.' Q: Who discovered the plant?

steps.action: Recall the anchored noun: Dr. Rao.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Sequence
workedTypes.idea: Recall the order of events. What makes it different: 'after/before' phrasing.
workedTypes.problem: 'He studied, then interned, then got hired.' Q: What did he do right before being hired?

steps.action: Retrieve the sequence: study → intern → hired.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Number/figure
workedTypes.idea: Recall a quantity. What makes it different: options change the digits.
workedTypes.problem: 'The bridge is 1,200 m long and took 3 years.' Q: How long is the bridge?

steps.action: Recall the figure tied to 'long': 1,200 m.
steps.why: Match the number to its label.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Main point
workedTypes.idea: Recall the gist. What makes it different: it's the whole passage, one line.
workedTypes.problem: A passage describes a village adopting solar power and cutting bills. Main point?

steps.action: Use the gist line: village switched to solar, saved money.
Pick the option matching that scope.

workedTypes.answer: The village adopted solar power and reduced costs.

traps: Swapped-detail options that change one name, date or number.
Reversing the order of events in a sequence question.
Guessing a shaky fact instead of a quick glance back.
Answering the main-point question with a minor detail.
learn.memoryHook: Read once, anchor names-numbers-order, hold one gist line, and recheck anything shaky.

crack.oneLine: Anchor the facts as you read; answer from memory but verify names, numbers and order in a five-second glance.

strategies.name: Active anchoring
strategies.method: Mentally 'highlight' each name, number and order-word on the first read.
strategies.howToApply: Pause a beat at every proper noun and digit; picture it. This makes recall automatic.
strategies.example: 'Anil… ₹5 lakh… then approved' → three anchors set for later questions.
strategies.whenFails: Abstract main-point questions — use the gist line instead.

strategies.name: Gist-first for main point
strategies.method: Frame one summary line immediately after reading.
strategies.howToApply: Say 'this passage is mainly about ___' in 6–8 words; use it for the main-idea question.
strategies.example: 'village went solar, cut bills' answers the main-point question directly.
strategies.whenFails: Specific figure questions — those need the anchored number.

strategies.name: Five-second verify
strategies.method: For any option you're under 90% sure of, glance back to the exact line.
strategies.howToApply: Scan for the keyword (name/number), read that one sentence, confirm or reject.
strategies.whenWorks: Close options that differ by one detail.
strategies.whenFails: When time is nearly out — trust your strongest anchor.

signalGuide.use: Retrieve the anchored number or date.

signalGuide.signal: two options differ by one word
signalGuide.use: Swapped-detail trap — verify against the text.

drill.question: What three things do you anchor while reading?
How do you answer the main-point question fast?
Two options differ by one number — likely a…?
Unsure of a fact — guess or glance back?

crack.whyItWorks: Recall questions reward attention at read-time, not re-reading. Anchoring concrete facts and holding a gist line means the answers are already in your head; a quick verify defeats the one-detail traps.
bestFor: Fact, number and sequence questions
Short passages read under time pressure
notFor: Deep inference or tone analysis
Passages you skim without anchoring anything
crack.practicePattern: Read once actively → anchor names/numbers/order + gist line → answer → verify shaky facts.

topics.description: Analyse arguments — assumptions, strengthen, weaken and conclude.

learn.realLife: Critical reasoning is what a good lawyer or a sharp friend does when someone claims 'we should do X because Y'. You don't accept it blindly — you ask 'what is being assumed?', 'does the evidence really support it?', 'what would break this argument?'. Exams give you a short argument and ask you to find its assumption, strengthen it, weaken it, or draw a safe conclusion.
learn.coreIdea: Every argument has a CONCLUSION (the claim) supported by PREMISES (the reasons), and usually a hidden ASSUMPTION (an unstated link the argument needs). Critical reasoning questions test whether you can separate these parts and reason about them: an assumption is what must be true for the argument to hold; strengthen adds support; weaken attacks the link; a conclusion is what the premises guarantee.
learn.howItWorks: Method: (1) Find the CONCLUSION — the main claim (often after 'therefore', 'so', 'hence', or it's the sentence others support). (2) Find the PREMISES — the reasons given. (3) Spot the GAP — what must be assumed to get from premises to conclusion? (4) Match the question: Assumption = the necessary hidden link (negate it and the argument collapses); Strengthen = new fact that supports the link; Weaken = new fact that attacks the link; Inference/Conclusion = only what the premises force, nothing extra. (5) Stay within the argument — don't bring outside opinions.

rules.name: Conclusion vs premise
rules.rule: The conclusion is the claim; premises are the reasons for it.
rules.explanation: Ask 'what is the author trying to convince me of?' — that's the conclusion; the 'because' parts are premises.
rules.example: 'Sales rose because we advertised' → conclusion: ads caused the rise; premise: sales rose after ads.

rules.name: Assumption = necessary link
rules.rule: An assumption is an unstated fact the argument needs to be true.
rules.explanation: Use the negation test: if denying the option destroys the argument, it's the assumption.
rules.example: 'Ads raised sales' assumes nothing else (season, price) caused the rise.

rules.name: Weaken attacks the link
rules.rule: To weaken, break the connection between premise and conclusion — don't attack a random detail.
rules.explanation: Show an alternative cause or a counter-case that makes the conclusion less likely.
rules.example: Weaken 'ads raised sales' → 'a festival that week boosted all shops'.

rules.name: Strengthen supports the link
rules.rule: To strengthen, add a fact that makes the conclusion more likely or rules out alternatives.
rules.explanation: Confirm the cause or eliminate a rival explanation.
rules.example: Strengthen 'ads raised sales' → 'sales stayed flat in a similar city with no ads'.

rules.name: Conclusion stays inside the premises
rules.rule: A valid conclusion uses only what's given — no extra assumptions, no extreme claims.
rules.explanation: Reject options with new information or absolute words the argument doesn't support.
rules.example: From 'most passed' you cannot conclude 'everyone will always pass'.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Find the assumption
workedTypes.idea: Identify the hidden necessary link. What makes it different: use the negation test.
workedTypes.problem: 'The new medicine cured 80% in trials, so it should be sold nationwide.' Assumption?

steps.action: Conclusion: sell nationwide; premise: 80% cured in trials.
Assumes trial results apply to the whole population (safe, representative).

workedTypes.answer: That the trial results generalise safely to everyone.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Weaken
workedTypes.idea: Attack the premise-conclusion link. What makes it different: find an alternative cause.
workedTypes.problem: 'Crime fell after CCTV was installed, so CCTV reduced crime.' Which weakens it most?

steps.action: Option: 'police also doubled patrols then' → alternative cause.

workedTypes.answer: Police patrols doubled at the same time (rival cause).

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Strengthen
workedTypes.idea: Support the link. What makes it different: rule out alternatives or confirm the cause.
workedTypes.problem: 'Students who sleep 8 hrs score higher, so sleep boosts scores.' Which strengthens it?

steps.action: Option: 'controlling for study hours, the effect held' → removes a rival cause.

workedTypes.answer: The effect held even after controlling for study time.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Draw the conclusion
workedTypes.idea: State only what premises force. What makes it different: no extra info, no extremes.
workedTypes.problem: 'All A are B. All B are C.' What follows?

traps: Confusing the conclusion with a premise.
Weakening by attacking an irrelevant detail instead of the main link.
Choosing an assumption that is 'nice to have' rather than necessary (failing the negation test).
Drawing a conclusion with extreme words (all/always/never) the argument never justified.
learn.memoryHook: Split conclusion vs premises, find the gap; assumption = negate-and-it-breaks, weaken = attack the link, conclude = stay inside.

crack.oneLine: Find the conclusion, find the gap between it and the premises — then the assumption/strengthen/weaken answer is about that gap.

strategies.name: Negation test for assumptions
strategies.method: Negate each option; the one that destroys the argument is the assumption.
strategies.howToApply: Add 'not' to the option — if the conclusion no longer follows, it's necessary → correct.
strategies.example: 'Trial applies to all' negated → 'does not apply' → argument collapses → it's the assumption.
strategies.whenWorks: Assumption and 'the argument depends on' questions.
strategies.whenFails: Strengthen/weaken — those add new facts, not necessary ones.

strategies.name: Find the alternative cause
strategies.method: To weaken, look for a rival explanation; to strengthen, rule one out.
strategies.howToApply: Ask 'what else could cause the result?'. Weaken introduces it; strengthen eliminates it.
strategies.example: CCTV/crime → weaken with 'extra patrols'; strengthen with 'no patrol change'.
strategies.whenFails: Pure logic (all/some) arguments — use set reasoning instead.

strategies.name: Stay-inside test for conclusions
strategies.method: Reject any option adding new info or extreme words.
strategies.howToApply: Keep only what the premises guarantee; delete 'always/never/all' unless premises are that strong.
strategies.whenFails: Assumption questions — those are unstated, not stated.

signalGuide.use: Marks the conclusion — the rest are premises.
Assumption question — apply the negation test.
Attack the link — find a rival cause or counter-case.
Add a fact confirming the link or ruling out alternatives.
Inference — stay strictly inside the premises.

drill.question: Which word usually flags the conclusion?
How do you test an assumption option?
drill.answer: Negate it — if the argument breaks, it's the assumption.

drill.question: Best way to weaken a causal claim?
Can a valid conclusion add new facts?

crack.whyItWorks: Every argument stands on a link from premises to conclusion. Assumption, strengthen and weaken questions are all about that single link — locate the gap and the right option is the one that fills, supports or breaks it.
bestFor: Assumption, strengthen, weaken and conclusion questions
Cause-and-effect arguments
notFor: Bringing in outside opinions or facts
Picking 'true but irrelevant' options
crack.practicePattern: Mark conclusion → list premises → find the gap → apply negation (assumption) or alternative-cause (strengthen/weaken) → confirm it stays inside the argument.

groups.description: Everyday workplace English — the right tone and clear emails.

topics.description: Tell formal from informal tone and rewrite appropriately.

learn.realLife: You already switch registers every day: you text a friend 'wanna hang out?' but you'd write a professor 'Would it be possible to meet?'. Formal vs informal English is just choosing the right outfit for the situation — casual clothes for friends, formal wear for an interview. Exams and workplaces test whether you can tell the two apart and rewrite one as the other.
learn.coreIdea: Register is the level of formality of language. Informal English is relaxed — contractions (I'm, don't), slang (kids, stuff, guy), phrasal verbs (find out, put off), and short chatty sentences. Formal English is polished — full forms (I am, do not), precise words (children, matter, gentleman), single-word verbs (discover, postpone), and complete, courteous sentences. The task is to identify the register or convert a sentence to the other.
