learn.howItWorks: To judge or convert register, check five things: (1) Contractions — informal uses them, formal spells out. (2) Word choice — informal 'get/kids/stuff' vs formal 'receive/children/items'. (3) Phrasal verbs — informal 'put off' vs formal 'postpone'. (4) Tone words — informal 'Hey/Thanks a lot' vs formal 'Dear/Thank you'. (5) Structure — informal is short and may drop words ('Coming?'); formal is complete and often uses polite/passive forms ('Could you please…', 'It was decided…'). Convert by swapping each informal marker for its formal twin (or vice-versa) without changing the meaning.

rules.name: Contractions signal informal
rules.rule: Formal writing avoids contractions; write the full form.
rules.explanation: I'm → I am, don't → do not, can't → cannot. Contractions feel conversational.
rules.example: Informal: 'We can't come.' → Formal: 'We are unable to attend.'

rules.name: Prefer precise single words
rules.rule: Formal English replaces vague/casual words with precise ones.
rules.explanation: get→receive, kids→children, stuff→items/matters, big→significant.
rules.example: Informal: 'Send me the stuff.' → Formal: 'Please send me the documents.'

rules.name: Single verb over phrasal verb
rules.rule: Formal prefers a one-word verb to a phrasal verb.
rules.explanation: put off→postpone, find out→discover, ask for→request, go up→increase.
rules.example: Informal: 'They put off the meeting.' → Formal: 'They postponed the meeting.'

rules.name: Polite, complete structures
rules.rule: Formal uses courteous, complete sentences and often polite modals or passive voice.
rules.explanation: 'Could you please…', 'We would appreciate…', 'It was decided…' sound formal.
rules.example: Informal: 'Gimme a reply soon.' → Formal: 'I would appreciate an early reply.'

rules.name: No slang or emojis in formal
rules.rule: Drop slang, fillers and emojis for formal register.
rules.explanation: 'guys', 'gonna', 'lol', 'ASAP', '!!!' break formality.
rules.example: Informal: 'Hey guys, sort this ASAP!' → Formal: 'Please resolve this at the earliest.'

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Identify the register
workedTypes.idea: Label a sentence formal or informal. What makes it different: spot the markers.
workedTypes.problem: 'Can't make it, gotta run!' — formal or informal?

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Informal → formal
workedTypes.idea: Rewrite casually-worded text formally. What makes it different: swap each marker.
workedTypes.problem: Make formal: 'Thanks a lot for helping us out with the stuff.'

steps.action: 'Thanks a lot'→'Thank you'; 'helping us out'→'assisting us'; 'stuff'→'matter'.
Combine: 'Thank you for assisting us with the matter.'

workedTypes.answer: Thank you for assisting us with the matter.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Formal → informal
workedTypes.idea: Relax an over-formal sentence. What makes it different: add contractions, simpler words.
workedTypes.problem: Make informal: 'I would be grateful if you could inform me.'

steps.action: Simplify: 'inform me'→'let me know'; add contraction.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Choose the appropriate one
workedTypes.idea: Pick the right register for a situation. What makes it different: match audience.
workedTypes.problem: Emailing a company's HR — which fits? (a) 'Hey, u free tmrw?' (b) 'Are you available tomorrow for a discussion?'

steps.action: (a) is slang/texting; (b) is complete and polite.

workedTypes.answer: (b) Are you available tomorrow for a discussion?

traps: Leaving a contraction (it's, don't) in a 'make it formal' answer.
Making it so formal it becomes unnatural or wordy.
Keeping phrasal verbs (find out, put off) in formal text.
Ignoring the audience — formal words in a friendly chat sound stiff and vice-versa.
learn.memoryHook: Formal = full forms + precise words + single verbs + polite & complete; informal = contractions + casual words + phrasal verbs + short.

crack.oneLine: Count the casual markers (contractions, slang, phrasal verbs) — many = informal; swap each for its formal twin to convert.

strategies.name: Marker count
strategies.method: Scan for contractions, slang and phrasal verbs to label register instantly.
strategies.howToApply: Two or more casual markers → informal; none → formal.
strategies.whenFails: Borderline neutral sentences — judge by audience/purpose.

strategies.name: Swap-table conversion
strategies.method: Replace each informal marker with its formal equivalent (and vice-versa).
strategies.howToApply: Contraction→full form, casual noun→precise noun, phrasal→single verb, 'Hey/Thanks'→'Dear/Thank you'.
strategies.whenFails: When meaning would change — keep the sense, only the tone shifts.

strategies.name: Audience check
strategies.method: Pick register by who receives the message.
strategies.example: Emailing HR → choose the complete, polite option.
strategies.whenFails: When both options match the audience — choose the clearer, correct one.

signalGuide.use: Lean informal — swap for a single verb in formal.

drill.question: Two+ casual markers means the sentence is…?

crack.whyItWorks: Register lives in a small set of markers — contractions, word choice, phrasal verbs, politeness and length. Counting or swapping those markers reliably identifies or converts the tone without touching meaning.
bestFor: Identify-register and rewrite questions
Choosing tone for a given audience
notFor: Changing the meaning while changing tone
Over-formalising until it sounds unnatural
crack.practicePattern: Scan markers → label register → swap each marker to the target tone → re-read for natural, correct English.

learn.realLife: The first email you send a recruiter or manager quietly decides how seriously they take you. A clear subject, a polite greeting, one focused message, and a clean sign-off make you look organised and reliable — while 'hey pls send' with no subject does the opposite. Professional email is a skill you'll use on day one of any job.
learn.coreIdea: A professional email has a predictable structure — Subject, Greeting, Opening line, Body (the purpose), a clear Ask/Call-to-action, Closing, and Signature — written in a polite, formal register. The exam (and real life) tests whether you can order these parts correctly, choose the right tone, write a good subject line, and make a specific, courteous request.
learn.howItWorks: Write in this order: (1) SUBJECT — short and specific ('Leave request: 12–14 Aug'), never blank. (2) GREETING — 'Dear Mr Rao,' / 'Hello Team,'. (3) OPENING — one line of context ('I hope you are well. I am writing regarding…'). (4) BODY — the message in 2–4 tight sentences, one topic. (5) ASK — a clear, polite call to action ('Could you please approve…?'). (6) CLOSING — 'Thank you for your time.' (7) SIGN-OFF + SIGNATURE — 'Regards, Anita Sharma'. Keep it formal: no slang, no ALL CAPS, one idea per email, and proofread.

rules.name: Always write a specific subject
rules.rule: The subject must summarise the email in a few words.
rules.explanation: It helps the reader prioritise and find the mail later; blank/vague subjects look careless.
rules.example: Good: 'Invoice #482 — payment follow-up'; Bad: '(no subject)' or 'Hi'.

rules.name: Greet and sign off properly
rules.rule: Open with a greeting and close with a sign-off + your name.
rules.explanation: 'Dear …,' to open and 'Regards, <Name>' to close frame the email politely.
rules.example: Dear Ms Iyer, … Regards, Rahul Verma.

rules.name: One purpose, stated early
rules.rule: Say why you're writing in the first two lines and keep to one topic.
rules.explanation: Busy readers want the point fast; mixing topics causes things to be missed.
rules.example: 'I am writing to request leave from 12–14 August.'

rules.name: Make the ask explicit and polite
rules.rule: End the body with a clear, courteous call to action.
rules.explanation: Use polite modals: 'Could you please…', 'Would it be possible to…'.
rules.example: 'Could you please confirm whether this is approved?'

rules.name: Keep it formal and proofread
rules.rule: No slang, emojis or ALL CAPS; check spelling and grammar before sending.
rules.explanation: Errors and casual tone reduce credibility in a professional setting.
rules.example: Avoid 'ASAP!!!' and 'thx' — use 'at the earliest' and 'Thank you'.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Order the parts
workedTypes.idea: Arrange email components correctly. What makes it different: fixed sequence.
workedTypes.problem: Order: (P) Body, (Q) Subject, (R) Greeting, (S) Sign-off.

steps.action: Subject first, then greeting, then body, then sign-off.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Best subject line
workedTypes.idea: Choose the clearest subject. What makes it different: specific + short.
workedTypes.problem: For a leave email, best subject? (a) 'Hi' (b) 'Leave request: 12–14 Aug' (c) '(none)' (d) 'IMPORTANT!!!'

steps.action: (b) is specific; others vague or shouty.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Fix the tone
workedTypes.idea: Rewrite a rude/casual line professionally. What makes it different: polite modal + formal words.
workedTypes.problem: Improve: 'Send the report now.'

steps.action: → 'Could you please share the report at your earliest convenience?'

workedTypes.answer: Could you please share the report at your earliest convenience?

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Right greeting/closing
workedTypes.idea: Pick appropriate open/close. What makes it different: match formality.
workedTypes.problem: Emailing an unknown official — greeting? (a) 'Hey!' (b) 'Dear Sir/Madam,'

traps: Sending with no subject or a vague one like 'Hi'.
Being blunt/demanding instead of polite ('Send now' vs 'Could you please send…').
Cramming several unrelated requests into one email.
Casual sign-offs or none — always close with 'Regards, <your name>'.
learn.memoryHook: Subject → Greeting → Opening → Body → Ask → Thanks → Sign-off: specific, polite, one purpose, proofread.

crack.oneLine: Follow the fixed skeleton — specific subject, greeting, one-purpose body, polite ask, sign-off — and keep the tone formal.

strategies.name: Skeleton recall
strategies.method: Reconstruct the standard order to solve arrange/complete questions.
strategies.howToApply: Recite Subject→Greeting→Opening→Body→Ask→Closing→Signature and map parts to it.
strategies.example: Given jumbled parts, place each on the skeleton → correct sequence.
strategies.whenFails: Tone questions — those need the politeness swap, not the order.

strategies.name: Specific-subject pick
strategies.method: Choose the subject that is short AND names the topic.
strategies.howToApply: Reject blank, 'Hi', and ALL-CAPS/!!! options; keep the concise, descriptive one.
strategies.whenFails: When two options are both specific — pick the more concise/neutral.

strategies.name: Politeness upgrade
strategies.method: Turn commands into polite requests with a modal + courtesy.
strategies.howToApply: 'Do X' → 'Could you please do X?'; add 'Thank you' and a greeting/sign-off.
strategies.example: 'Send report now' → 'Could you please share the report today? Thank you.'
strategies.whenFails: When the line is already polite — don't over-soften.

signalGuide.use: Weak subject — pick the specific, calm one.
Too blunt — upgrade to 'Could you please…'.
Split — keep one purpose per email.

drill.question: Polite version of 'Send it now'?
drill.answer: Could you please send it at the earliest?

drill.question: How many main purposes per email?

crack.whyItWorks: Professional emails follow a fixed, expected skeleton with a formal register. Knowing the skeleton answers structure questions, and the politeness/subject rules answer tone and subject questions — there's little to guess.
bestFor: Order/complete-the-email questions
Subject-line and tone-fix questions
notFor: Long, multi-topic emails
Casual tone with professional recipients
crack.practicePattern: Map parts to the skeleton → pick a specific subject → keep one purpose → upgrade politeness → add greeting & sign-off → proofread.
